coredata.dc:description: Novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified PbBiO2Cl nanosheets, 0D/2D hybird photocatalyst has been firstly prepared via the reactive ionic liquid 1-hexadecyl-3 methylimidazolium chlorine assisted facile solvothermal approach. PbBiO2Cl nanosheets were designed to shorten the migration distance from bulk phase to the surface for the charge carriers, while CQDs was introduced to accelerate the transfer of surface charge carriers as well as activate molecular oxygen. Various techniques were conducted to explore structures, morphologies, optical and electrochemical properties of the as-prepared materials. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl materials were appraised via the photodegradation of antibiotic agent ciprofloxacin (CIP), tetracycline (TC) and endocrine disrupter bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light irradiation. The results demonstrated that the photocatalytic activities of CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl materials has a significantly enhancement compared to pure PbBiO2Cl nanosheets and the 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl material displays the highest performance. In addition, electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments and radicals quenching experiments were employed to prove that the superoxide radicals and holes were the main active species during the photocatalytic process. Based on the above results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was put forward. This research aims to develop more hybrid photocatalysts and hope to make progress in environmental remediation.

XPS spectra of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite. (a) Survey of the samples; (b) Pb 4f; (c) Bi 4f; (d) O 1s; (e) Cl 2p and (f) C 1s.
Low magnification TEM images (a–b) of PbBiO2Cl and low magnification TEM image (c) and high magnification TEM image (d) of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore-size distribution curves (inset) of (a) pure PbBiO2Cl and (b) 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of CIP in the presence of the as-prepared PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites with different loading amounts of CQDs and (b) kinetic fits for the degradation of CIP with the as-prepared PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites with different loading amounts of CQDs. Photocatalytic degradation of (c) TC and (d) BPA in the as-prepared PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites with diverse weight ratios of CQDs.
PL spectra of PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite.
(a) Transient photocurrent response PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite; (b) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite.
ESR spectra of the DMPO-O2 •− (a) adducts and DMPO-•OH (b) recorded with PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite under visible light irradiation.
Comparison of photocatalytic activities of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite for the degradation of CIP with or without adding IPA, t-butanol, TEA, N2, and AO under visible light irradiation.
Possible reaction mechanism of pollutants degradation over CQDs/PbBiO2Cl photocatalyst under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl nanosheets with improved molecular oxygen activation ability for photodegradation of organic contaminants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified PbBiO2Cl nanosheets, 0D/2D hybird photocatalyst has been firstly prepared via the reactive ionic liquid 1-hexadecyl-3 methylimidazolium chlorine assisted facile solvothermal approach. PbBiO2Cl nanosheets were designed to shorten the migration distance from bulk phase to the surface for the charge carriers, while CQDs was introduced to accelerate the transfer of surface charge carriers as well as activate molecular oxygen. Various techniques were conducted to explore structures, morphologies, optical and electrochemical properties of the as-prepared materials. Furthermore, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl materials were appraised via the photodegradation of antibiotic agent ciprofloxacin (CIP), tetracycline (TC) and endocrine disrupter bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light irradiation. The results demonstrated that the photocatalytic activities of CQDs modified PbBiO2Cl materials has a significantly enhancement compared to pure PbBiO2Cl nanosheets and the 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl material displays the highest performance. In addition, electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments and radicals quenching experiments were employed to prove that the superoxide radicals and holes were the main active species during the photocatalytic process. Based on the above results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was put forward. This research aims to develop more hybrid photocatalysts and hope to make progress in environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: With the swift development of urbanisation and industrialisation, the discharge of municipal sewage and industrial wastewater pose a great impact on the water environment. Traditional water treatment methods for the rapid remediation of water pollution [1,2], such as physical adsorption [3], chemical oxidation and microbial treatment [4], requires long reaction time and large investment. Consequently, it is urgent to develop a highly efficient and energy-saving environmental restoration technology to solve these problems [5–9]. Photocatalysis displays high efficiency and low energy consumption, meanwhile it eliminates secondary contamination [10–15]. Simultaneously, it was also recognized by the environmental protection community as a revolutionary breakthrough in the field of environmental purification in the 21 st century [16–20].

Among all the photocatalysts, the first to be reported in the literature was TiO2. However, the excitation wavelength of TiO2 is less than 388 nm, so it can only work in the ultraviolet region [21–23]. Recently, bismuth halide photocatalysts, a novel visible-light responsive semiconductor photocatalyst, were widely investigated owing to their special band gap structure, environmental genially fantastic chemical properties, inexpensive, fantastic chemical properties and so on [24–27]. In which, bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) has begun to attract more and more attention benefit from its unique layered structure formed with staggered [Bi2O2] layer and double halogen atomic layer, which can produce inside static electric fields that can accelerate efficiency of separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, resulting in the enhancement of photocatalytic performance [28–31]. However, BiOCl could only absorb ultraviolet light for its wide band gap, which limits its application in industry.

Lead (Pb), adjacent to the Bi element in the periodic table of the chemical element, the outer electronic structure is 6s26p2, similar to the outer electronic structure of Bi of 6s26p3. When the outer layer of Pb loses two electrons and formed Pb2+, the property was more stable. The atomic number of Bi is similar to Pb, leading the similar radius of Pb2+ and Bi3+. As reported, the crystal structure of the formed lead bismuth oxyhalides did not show substantial change compared with the bismuth oxyhalide. Simultaneously, lead bismuth oxyhalides (PbBiO2X) have many advantages, such as variety of shapes, excellent band gap and high secession effectiveness of electron-hole pairs [32–34], accordingly, many scholars dedicated to research the materials that Bi3+ was partial replaced by Pb2+. There are many traditional methods for synthesizing PbBiO2X photocatalysts, such as solvothermal method [35], solid phase reaction method [36], and so on. So far, three kinds of PbBiO2X compounds, PbBiO2Cl, PbBiO2Br, and PbBiO2I, have been reported. Shan et al. [35] synthesized a fresh layered photocatalyst of Bi-based PbBiO2Cl according to solid-state reaction, which could degrade methyl orange (MO) higher than that of perovskite TiO2 under UV light and visible light illumination. Liu et al. [37] prepared PbBiO2Cl/BiOCl nanocomposite via controlled hydrothermal method, and its compositions and morphologies could be dominated by altering parameters, including Pb/Bi molar ratio, temperature and reaction pH. Above all, it can photodegrade crystal violet (CV) and 2-hydroxybenzoic acid (2-HBA) in aqueous solution. Yu’s group [38] synthesized three new single crystal PbBiO2X (X = Cl, Br and I) square-like nanoplates with a length of hundreds nanometers by simple hydrothermal method, which exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activities on degradation of organic pollutant, due to their fresh nanostructure and effective photoelectric variation. However, almost all the morphologies of PbBiO2X reported were mostly nanospheres. In this work, PbBiO2Cl nanosheets were first reported with excellent photocatalytic activity under visible light.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), new carbon-based zero-dimensional materials with a diameter of <10 nm, it may be quasi-spherical nanoparticles composed of dispersed spherical carbon particles, which can be sp2 carbon clusters filled in nanocrystals or sp3 carbon clusters fused in diamond-like structure [39,40]. Due to the wide range of raw materials, the uncomplicated operation during the preparation, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, CQDs have been extensively applied in numerous crucial fields such as medical imaging technology, environmental monitoring, chemical analysis, catalyst preparation, and energy exploitation [41–44]. Furthermore, CQDs have been successfully introduced into photocatalysts including Ag3PO4 [45], TiO2 [46–49], ZnO [50], Bi2WO6 [51,52], C3N4 [53], BiOX [54] to promote photocatalytic activity owing to their good electron transfer capability. It is well established that CQDs are very small in size and it can be in close contact with main photocatalysts to create nanoscale heterojunctions, which greatly accelerate the effective separation of electron-hole pairs [55–59]. Meanwhile, it can also increase the interface contact between CQDs and PbBiO2Cl nanosheets and establish bulk-to-surface passages for electrons, to accelerate transfer process of interfacial charge.

Herein, novel 0D/2D CQDs/PbBiO2Cl photocatalysts were successfully synthesized utilizing a facile solvothermal route for the first time and a series of characterization were employed to explore the composites in detail. In addition, three model contaminations, such as tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and bisphenol A (BPA), were adopted to examine the photocatalytic activities under visible light irradiation. Eventually, the main active species responsible for the photodegradation of three model contaminants were determined by electron spin resonance (ESR) investigation and radicals quenching experiments, and the photodegradation mechanism of pollutants was presented.

All materials were analytical graded and used without further purification. The ionic liquid [C16mim]Cl (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium chlorine) (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co.Ltd.

CQDs were obtained by freeze-drying with a facile one-step process. As well as the detail steps were as follows: 5 mmol (1.05 g) citric acid was poured into 10 mL deionized water and then 335 μL ethylenediamine was dripped. The commixture was flowed into 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h, then cooled down to room temperature. The terminal product was reddish-brown liquid, and the CQDs solution was prepared by dialysis for 24 h. Finally, by freeze-drying, the CQDs solid was obtained.

The CQDs/PbBiO2Cl materials were prepared according to a traditional solvent-thermal process. Firstly, 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, 1 mmol of Pb(NO3)2 and a given amount of CQDs were added together into a beaker and dissolved into 10 mL of H2O by ultrasonic and stirring treatment and defined as solution A. Secondly, 1 mmol of ionic liquid [C16mim]Cl was dissolved into 10 mL ethanol by ultrasonic treatment and defined as solution B. Eventually, solution B was poured into solution A dropwise by lasting stirring and stirred until the suspension became uniform, then poured into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave reactor and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, the obtained product was gradually cooled to room temperature and centrifuged, then remove impurities therein with deionized water and ethanol. According to the mass ratio of CQDs added, CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites were marked as 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt%, 8 wt%, respectively.
This part is in the supporting information.

In order to reconnoitre the photocatalytic performance of the procured PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites, three organic pollutants, including ciprofloxacin (CIP), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) and bisphenol A (BPA), were degraded as aim pollutants under visible light irradiation. It is worth noting that the photo source was a 300 W Xe-lamp with a 400 nm light-cut filter and the whole experimental process was actualized in a circulating water bath at 30 °C for preventing the possible generation of thermal catalysis in the experiment. Firstly, 0.03 of photocatalysts was dispersed into 100 mL of TC (20 mg L−1), 100 mL of CIP (10 mg L−1) and 100 mL of BPA (10 mg L−1) solutions, respectively. And before the irradiation, the suspension was continually stirred for nearly 30 min in the dark to ascertain adsorption-desorption equipoise. During the photodegradation process, approximate 4 mL of the suspension solution was continually taken out at given time intervals and centrifuged (13,500 rpm, 3 min) to eliminate the catalysts. Finally, the residual CIP or TC in the solution was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer at 276 and 356 nm, respectively. The amount of residuary BPA was determined by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).

As depicted in Fig. 1 , the composition and crystal structure of PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites with diverse weight ratios of CQDs were confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Additionally, the whole diffraction peaks of the obtained samples could be chiefly indexed to the orthorhombic phase structure of PbBiO2Cl (JCPDS No. 13-0352). A weak peak at 14° was ascertained in the XRD pattern for pure CQDs in Fig. S1 [11]. But no evident peaks ascribed to CQDs were detected in Fig. 1, which may be due to the low amount of CQDs dopants or their high dispersity [40,51,54]. The results showed that the CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites were single-phase and high purity because there was not any impurity diffraction peaks after CQDs were added. And the diffraction peaks of the PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites located at 2θ = 14.3°, 23.6°, 29.0°, 31.2°, 32.2°, 43.7°, 46.0°, 52.4°, 55.3°, 56.8°, and 76.0°, corresponding to the (002), (111), (004), (113), (020), (006), (220), (131), (026), (117), and (137) crystal planes, respectively.

FT-IR was followed out to further analyze the chemical bonds and molecular structures of PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites with diverse weight ratios of CQDs. Amongst them, two less obvious absorption peaks at 1390 cm−1 and 1570 cm-1 for CQDs belong to CO and −COO– stretching oscillation in Fig. S2, indicating that the surface of the CQDs has the presence of a −COOH group [60]. As shown in Fig. 2 , there were apparent absorption peaks at 537 cm-1, 1385 cm-1 and 1577 cm-1 of the as-prepared PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites. The peaks at 537 cm-1 was the characteristic absorption peak of Bi-O stretching vibratio in PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites, which can be found in the infrared spectrum of PbBiO2Cl. The absorption peaks at 1385 cm-1 and 1577 cm-1 were slightly offset compared with pure CQDs, can belong to the −COO– and CO stretching oscillation in the PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites [54]. In addition, there were no any characteristic peaks or other peaks about ionic liquid [C16mim]Cl, indicating that ionic liquid [C16mim]Cl was easy to be removed during the synthesis process. The above results indicated that CQDs has been successfully compounded onto PbBiO2Cl during solvothermal synthesis process.

Further analysis of the surface chemical constituents and chemical form of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite was researched by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3
a is a full spectrum of the sample, which precisely indicated that 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite were principally comprised of Bi, Cl, Pb, O and C elements. In addition, in order to further ascertain the valence state of each element in the material, XPS high-resolution map analysis were shown in Fig. 3 (b–f). In the midst of Figures, the XPS spectrum of Pb exhibited that the two forceful peaks at 138.0 and 143.0 eV were uniform with the conjuncting energies of Pb2+ in 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite in Fig. 3 b [61]. In Fig. 3c, two forceful peaks located at 159.0 and 164.2 eV can be accredit to Bi 4f 5/2 and Bi 4f 7/2, denoting the Bi3+ in the crystal constructure [62,63]. In 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites, due to the interaction between PbBiO2Cl and CQDs, the Bi 4f peak displayed a tiny offset parallelled to the Bi 4f peak in pure PbBiO2Cl materials. The peak of O 1s at 529.7eV (Fig. 3d) belonged to the O element in the 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl material. Fig. 3 e showed the high-resolution Cl 2p peak, the peaks at 198.0 eV and 199.7 eV were consistent with the presence of Cl in the form of Cl− in the 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl material [64]. As depicted in Fig. 3f, the photoelectron spectra of C 1s revealed the functional groups on the surface of CQDs. Among them, the forceful peak at nearly 285.0 eV was attributed to the CC bond, and the peaks kerneled at the conjuncting energies of 284.0 and 286.0 eV [65]. The results of XPS analysis verified the coexistence of PbBiO2Cl and CQDs in the CQDs/PbBiO2Cl materials.

For better to observe the morphologies and microstructures of PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were utilized. PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites were larger irregular sheet material and not thick in thickness, which was seen in Fig. S3. Fig. 4
a and Fig. 4 b showed typical TEM images of the PbBiO2Cl, it could be observed that PbBiO2Cl had a typical sheet structure and the as-prepared PbBiO2Cl material were made up of nanosheets with sizes of approximately hundreds of nanometers. And the thickness of pure PbBiO2Cl nanosheets was about 10–15 nm. Furthermore, Fig. 4 c displayed typical TEM images of the 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite, and it can be found that some regions were suspected to be CQDs. Among them, the near pellucidity of the sheets implied that the thickness of the nanosheets was very thin, only about 5 nm. Other than this, high resolution transmission (HRTEM) analysis of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites was carried out in Fig. 4 d. The (020) crystal faces of PbBiO2Cl material and (006) crystal faces of CQDs were determined, and the lattice spacing was 0.28 nm and 0.21 nm, respectively [54]. And there were some teeny nanodots uniformly attached to the surface of PbBiO2Cl nanosheets, which proving that CQDs has been successfully loaded on the surface of PbBiO2Cl nanosheets with a good combination.

To investigate the influence of the surface area and porousness on the photocatalysts, the specific surface areas and pore sizes of PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites were determined by nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms. Fig. 5
a showed the surface area of pure PbBiO2Cl material was measured to be 6.95 m2/g. However, when CQDs were introduced, the specific surface area of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl was measured to be 20.54 m2/g in Fig. 5b, which was three times higher than that of pure PbBiO2Cl material. The larger the specific surface area were and the more active sites were exposed, as everyone knows, the better the photocatalytic activity of the material was. The above results signified that the combination of CQDs increased the specific surface area of pure PbBiO2Cl material, increased the diffusion of pollutants, so that pollutants could be adsorbed on the surface of PbBiO2Cl material, causing higher photocatalytic activity. [66]

The photocatalytic activity of acquired PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites with diverse weight ratios of CQDs was firstly assessed in this research by photodegrading the colorless antibiotic reagents CIP under visible light irradiation. Ciprofloxacin (CIP) is a kind of drug which is difficult to be metabolized by the human body and seriously endangers the health of the human body. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the photodegradation experiments obviously revealed that only a small quantity of CIP was removed under visible light irradiation without any photocatalyst. When the PbBiO2Cl was added, 47.1% of the CIP could be photodegraded in the course of 75 min. Then, with the introduction of CQDs, the photocatalytic activity of PbBiO2Cl was notably enhanced. 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite showed the best photocatalytic performance, and 78.9% of the CIP had been photodegraded in the course of 75 min. However, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite decreased until the CQDs content is higher than 3 wt%. That is because too many CQDs will cause the PbBiO2Cl surface to be obscured by the incident visible photons, resulting in poor light exposure of the photogenerated carriers produced by the PbBiO2Cl [51,67]. In addition, we also investigated the photodegradation kinetics of CIP by the photocatalysts, and discovered that the variation of CIP concentration on PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl materials with reaction time obeyed the quasi-first-order kinetic curve of −ln(C/C0) = kt, as shown in Fig. 6 b. The 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite has the supreme photodegradation rate, which is nearly 2.3, 1.5, 1.1 and 1.2 times higher than that of PbBiO2Cl, 0.5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl, 5 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl, 8 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl.

Meantime, TC and BPA were also used to test the photocatalytic activity of procured PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites with diverse weight ratios of CQDs. Fig. 6 c and Fig. 6 d showed the effect of as-prepared PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites on the photodegradation of TC and BPA, respectively. When the pollutant is a colorless antibiotic TC, it was observed that the photodegradation effect of TC without any catalyst was negligible. The photodegradation efficiency of PbBiO2Cl to TC is only 60.4%. Then the CQDs are compounded, the photocatalytic degradation activity of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites has a significant improvement. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the photodegradation rates of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite to TC with contents of 0.5 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt% and 8 wt% were 72.9%, 79.8%, 74.3% and 74.1%, respectively. The photocatalyst with 3 wt% CQDs has the best effect on the photodegradation of TC. In addition, as shown in Fig. 6d, after 6 h of visible light exposure, 69.7% of BPA had been photodegraded with pure PbBiO2Cl. And it can be observed that 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites still has the best photocatalytic degradation activity, 92% of BPA was degraded within 6 h. The photodegradation rates of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite to BPA with contents of 0.5 wt%, 5 wt% and 8 wt% were 84.2%, 90.3% and 87.1%, respectively. The relevant coefficients and pseudo-first-order constants are sumed up in Table S1. The above results indicated that CQDs/PbBiO2Cl materials can effectively intensify the photocatalytic activity of pure PbBiO2Cl materials under visible light.

Moreover, cyclic experiments were carried out to testify the reusability and stableness of the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. S5, the experiment about photocatalytic degradation of CIP with 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite was carried out. After 5 consecutive cycles, the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation remained basically unchanged, which proved that the stability of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite was excellent. In addition, the crystal structure of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite was determined by XRD (Fig. S6), which proved that there was no obvious change of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite, indicating the CQDs/PbBiO2Cl was stable during the photodegradation process.

UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was employed to test the light absorption properties of procured PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites and the result was shown in Fig. S7. The light absorption edge of PbBiO2Cl is around 490 nm. After the introduction of CQDs, the absorption of the composites in the visible region is obviously enhanced, which may ascribed to the interaction between PbBiO2Cl and CQDs. And as the amount of CQDs gradually increased, the absorption range the visible-light harvesting capability of the acquired samples in the visible region was also enhanced. Hence, the results indicated that the introduction of CQDs could broaden the light absorption range of PbBiO2Cl efficiently, resulting in more photogenerated carriers and increased photocatalytic performance under visible light [68,69].

Then photoluminescence (PL) spectra were applied to research how the photoinduced electrons and holes of PbBiO2Cl and CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites were transferred and recombined. In Fig. 7 , it was observed both PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites showed a powerful peak located at 465 nm while 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites has a lower fluorescence intensity than PbBiO2Cl, which indicated that the separation of CQDs can inhibit the coincidence of photogenerated carriers significantly. This phenomenon may be due to the efficient and rapid transfer of electrons from the surface of PbBiO2Cl to CQDs, which improved the secession efficiency of photogenerated carriers [63,70].

The photocatalytic performance of semiconductor catalysts mainly depends on the migration and secession efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Consequently, electrochemical analysis was used to further explore the electron secession efficiency of PbBiO2Cl material and the migration process of electrons from PbBiO2Cl surface to CQDs. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the momentary photocurrent response of PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites was measured under visible light and measured over several switching cycles. It was plainly observed that the photocurrent intensity of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites was about twice than that of PbBiO2Cl, the stronger photocurrent indicated higher photogenerated electron-hole pair secession efficiency, so the surface of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites can transfer electrons quickly and efficiently [71].

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), apart from transient photocurrent response, was also a effective characterization to study the charge transfer resistance. Mostly, the longer the diameter of the hemicycle in the Nyquist diagram, the larger the charge transfer resistance were. Fig. 8 b exhibited the Nyquist plot for the obtained pure PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites. It was discovered that 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites had a smaller resistance value contrast to PbBiO2Cl, which connoted that the rapid transformation of interface charges to electron acceptor happens and results in effective secession of electron-hole pairs. The consequences of EIS and photocurrents coincided precisely with the PL results, which confirmed that the separation of CQDs is an efficient method to improve photocatalytic performance [71–73].

Electron spin resonance (ESR), also named Paramagnetic Resonance, is a resonance transition between magnetic energy levels of an electron spin magnetic moment in a constant magnetic field under the action of radio frequency electromagnetic field, which was executed to research the active species engenderd by PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite in the course of the photodegradation process [74]. As shown in Fig. 9 a, in the process of photocatalytic removal of organic pollutants, the methanol-dispersed PbBiO2Cl can be detected the typical signal peaks of DMPO-O2 •− during the photodegradation process, while the typical signal peaks of DMPO-O2 •- was not detected in the dark, indicated that O2 •− was generated during photocatalysis. Furthermore, the peak of DMPO-O2 •- of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite is about twice as high as that of PbBiO2Cl. However, Fig. 9 b showed that no signal of DMPO-•OH in the aqueous solution of PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite with no light or visible light, indicating that DMPO-•OH was not the main active species in this system.

In order to determine in detail the active species in the photocatalytic reaction of the 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite, the radicals quenching experiments was used to verify. Hence different scavengers were added, such as tert-butanol, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO) and Triethanolamine (TEOA). Among them, t-butanol and IPA were hydroxyl radical scavenger, N2 were quencher of O2 •−, holes (h+) can be scavenged by AO and TEOA [63,75]. As depicted in Fig. 10 , the photodegradation rate of CIP was almost constant after t-butanol and IPA were added. However, the photocatalytic degradation rate of CIP decreased significantly after N2, AO or TEOA. Consequently, according to the results of ESR and radicals quenching experiments, it was proved that h+ and O2 •− played a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation of 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite.

In addition, it is necessary to research the intrinsic causes of O2 •− and •OH in the process of photodegradation experiments of PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite. In Fig. S9 of the XPS valence band spectrum, it was clear that PbBiO2Cl and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite have a valence band at 1.49 V. And from DRS calculations (Fig. S8), the band gaps of the PbBiO2Cl were 2.65 V. In consequence, the minimum values of conduction band (CB) of PbBiO2Cl can be calculated to be -1.16 V. Hence, the CB values of PbBiO2Cl (- 1.16 V) were sufficient to reduce O2 to O2 •− because the redox potentials of ·OH/OH- was +2.38 V (vs. SHE) [76]. However, the VB values of PbBiO2Cl (1.49 V) was not adequate to oxidize •OH to OH−, that was because the redox potentials of O2/O2 •− was −0.046 V (vs. SHE) [77]. The analytical results of the XPS valence spectra were consistent with those of the ESR spectra and the radicals quenching experiments.

A possible reaction mechanism about photodegradation of pollutants by CQDs/PbBiO2Cl photocatalyst under visible light irradiation is proposed. As typical quasi-spherical nanoparticles, CQDs have a conjugated system, which causes CQDs to transfer electrons as an electron acceptor, thus prevent the recombination of electron-hole pairs. As depicted in Fig. 11 , the electrons in the VB of PbBiO2Cl were excited by visible light, and then jumped to the CB, accordingly leaving holes in the VB that can directly oxidize pollutants. At the same time, the photoelectrons, in the CB of PbBiO2Cl, transfer to the CQDs due to the excellent conductivity of CQDs, and then reduced the surface trapped O2 to O2 •−, thus, degrading the pollutant. In effect, CQDs can not only act as a catalyst, but also act as an adsorption center, fully adsorbing pollutants on its surface and trapping electrons to reduce O2 to O2 •−. The results of free radical trapping experiment and ESR analysis implied that O2 •− played a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation process, and CQDs could be used as the center of photocatalytic reaction.

In summary, PbBiO2Cl nanosheets has been successfully prepared via an effortless solvothermal method. Above all, CQDs can play a crucial role in promoting photocatalytic activity root in excellent electron transfer capability and enhanced light-harvesting competency. In consequence, a small amount of CQDs was added as modifier, and assembled on the PbBiO2Cl nanosheets to improve the photocatalytic performance. After the modifiaction of CQDs, the photodegradation activity of CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composites towards TC, CIP and BPA was obviously enhanced relatived to pure PbBiO2Cl material under visible light irradiation, and 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite displayed the supreme photocatalytic activity. Moreover, 3 wt% CQDs/PbBiO2Cl composite also had good stability and recyclability, and had great potential in environmental protection. In this system, superoxide radical and hole were determined to be main active species through ESR analysis and radicals quenching experiments. This work is expected to accelerate the design and development of new 0D/2D hybrid photocatalysts with excellent visible-light catalytic efficiency.
